title: Build Your Python Foundation: Environment, Thinking, and Your First Data Script

sections.title: Audit Your Current Environment
sections.prompt: Write down your Python setup right now: version, package manager, where packages live, how you'd revert if something breaks. If you don't have isolation (venv/conda), that's your first step — not optional. Why? The blog shows it prevents 'code works today, breaks next month' disasters.

sections.title: Decompose a Real Problem You Face
sections.prompt: Pick one actual data problem: analyzing a spreadsheet, understanding a dataset, processing logs, calculating metrics from records. Now apply decomposition: What's the big problem? Break it into 3-4 smaller pieces. Which piece solves first? Write this *before* opening Python.

sections.title: Write Your 'Hello Data' Script
sections.prompt: Create a Python script that: (1) loads real data (CSV, JSON, dict, API response), (2) calculates one meaningful result (average, count, sum, filter), (3) saves output. Run it. What data did you touch? What surprised you? This is computational thinking applied — not 'Hello World' noise.

sections.title: Break Your Code & Read the Error
sections.prompt: Introduce one deliberate bug (wrong key, typo, type mismatch). Run the script. Copy the exact error message here. Now decode it: What line? What went wrong? Why? This skill — reading errors — is what separates 'I learned Python' from 'I can debug Python.' Do this three times.

sections.title: Verify Your Foundation
sections.prompt: Before moving forward: Do you understand why environment isolation prevents future pain? Can you explain decomposition? Did your script produce real output from real data? If yes to all three, you've built the foundation. If not, which piece needs another cycle?

cta: Run your script. Reply with one error you hit and how you fixed it — that proves you're building the skill that compounds.
